北京（路透社） - 中国顶级乘车App下降瑞典时装零售商H＆amp;米从其列表中获集，因为中国名人在越来越多的外国标签中停止了在新疆强迫劳动的西方指责中越来越突兀。
  H＆amp; M在中国的社交媒体用户在中国展示了一份声明，该公司去年宣布宣布，在穆斯林的使用强迫劳动报告后，宣布宣布它将不再来自新疆的棉花。
 西方政府和权利团体在营地拘留和折磨难民主义者的野蛮地区指控当局，一些前囚犯表示他们遭到意识形态灌输。
 北京否认指责并将营地描述为有助于打击宗教极端主义的职业培训中心。
 搜索结果H＆amp; M在Didi Chuxing Ride-Hailing应用程序中，中国所有的主要城市都没有出现在周五的结果。该公司没有立即回复评论请求。
  反对H＆amp; M引起了中国电子商务巨头阿里巴巴集团控股有限公司，购物App Meituan和Search Engine Baidu Inc的地图应用程序从他们的列表中删除瑞典零售商。
 其他海外品牌，包括Burberry Group Plc，Nike Inc和Adidas AG，​​也面临着在线回来，以便在新疆采购棉花采购的类似陈述。 
H＆amp的人权部分Hmgroup.com星期五不再携带到新疆2020年陈述的联系。仍然可以通过页面的直接地址访问该声明。
 在Inditex，VF Corp，PVH和Abercrombie＆Amercomp＆amp的网站上看到的新疆强迫劳动的关注或不耐受的陈述。周四不再提供惠誉。
  通过路透社询问，VF CORP指出了其网站单独部分的声明，表示它没有来自新疆来源的。 Google缓存显示了过去四天中添加了该声明。 vf没有回复一个问题，询问为什么声明已被移动。
  “我们必须支持品牌，保留谴责奴隶制的陈述，羞辱那些将他们带走的人。这是这些品牌的定义时刻，“法国MEPRaphael Glucksmann说，由中国批准的社交媒体竞选活动呼吁零售商抵御新疆的强迫劳动。
 “欧洲的消费者需要对撤回其陈述的公司来说，请施加反压。”
   关于德国时尚屋Hugo Boss的中国微博账户的一条消息周四表示，它将“继续购买和支持新疆棉花”。雨果老板周五表示，它不是授权的帖子，并被删除相应。
 在周五的电子邮件中，公司发言人Carolin Westermann表示，在其网站上说明“迄今为止，Hugo Boss没有采购任何源自直接供应商的新疆的货物”是其官方立场。棉花排蔓延到娱乐世界中，中国名人丢弃了几个外国零售标签，包括六个美国品牌等耐克。 
耐克拥有的新余额，甲甲，Tommy Hilfiger和Confervers，在中国遭到宣称，宣称他们不会使用新疆棉花。
   “我可以确认Uniqlo的中国品牌大使已经终止了合同，”快速零售发言人表示。
  在过去的两天里，至少27名中国电影明星和歌手宣布他们将停止与国外品牌合作。
  他们的决定被中国互联网用户在岸上的互联网上得到了广泛赞扬的，因为在流行的Twitter的微博微博上。
 “我过去买了这些产品，这种情况并不意味着我现在将把它们扔掉，摧毁它们或类似的东西，”北京购物中心外的毕业生吕西刘说。
 “我会做的就是避免暂时购买它们。”
 除了时尚和零售业之外，中国周五批准了英国组织和个人，在英国施加自行制裁后的新疆，曾经被称为“谎言和不诚实”。 
“中国外交部说：”中国坚定地决心保障其国家主权，安全和发展利益，并警告英国方面不要进一步走向错误的道路，“中国外交部说。 “否则，中国将坚决进一步反应。”  制裁是伦敦与北京之间关系恶化的最新迹象，包括中国对香港前英国殖民地不同意的镇压，这在1997年返回中国统治时已经保证了其自由。

BEIJING (Reuters) -China’s top ride-hailing app dropped Swedish fashion retailer H&M from its listings as Chinese celebrities stopped endorsing foreign labels in a growing uproar over Western accusations of forced labour in Xinjiang.
  H&M faced a public backlash in China when social media users in the country circulated a statement the company made last year announcing it would no longer source cotton from Xinjiang after reports of the use of forced labour by Uighur Muslims.
 Western governments and rights groups have accused authorities in the farwestern region of detaining and torturing Uighurs in camps, where some former inmates have said they were subject to ideological indoctrination.
 Beijing denies the accusations and describes the camps in question as vocational training centres which help combat religious extremism.
 Search results for H&M in the Didi Chuxing ride-hailing app for all of China’s major cities yielded no results on Friday. The company did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
  The backlash against H&M caused Chinese e-commerce giant Alibaba Group Holding Ltd, shopping app Meituan and the maps app for search engine Baidu Inc to each remove the Swedish retailer from their listings.
 Other overseas brands, including Burberry Group PLC, Nike Inc, and Adidas AG have also faced an online blowback for making similar statements regarding their sourcing of cotton in Xinjiang.
 The Human Rights section of H&M’s website hmgroup.com on Friday no longer carried the link to the 2020 statement on Xinjiang. The statement could still be accessed through the page’s direct address.
 Statements expressing concern about or intolerance of forced labour in Xinjiang previously seen on the websites of Inditex, VF Corp, PVH and Abercrombie & Fitch were no longer available on Thursday.
  Following enquiries by Reuters, VF Corp pointed to a statement on a separate section of its website that said it did not source from Xinjiang. A Google cache showed the statement had been added in the last four days. VF did not respond to a question asking why the statement had been moved.
  “We have to stand by the brands keeping statements condemning slavery and shame those who are taking them down. This is a defining moment for these brands,” said French MEP Raphael Glucksmann, one of 10 EU individuals sanctioned by China who has run social media campaigns calling on retailers to stand against forced labour in Xinjiang.
 “Consumers in Europe need to place counter pressure on companies retracting their statements.”
   A message on the Chinese Weibo account of the German fashion house Hugo Boss said on Thursday that it would “continue to purchase and support Xinjiang cotton.” Hugo Boss said on Friday that it was not an authorized post, and had been deleted accordingly.
 In an email to Reuters on Friday, company spokeswoman Carolin Westermann said that an undated English-language statement on its website stating that “so far, HUGO BOSS has not procured any goods originating in the Xinjiang region from direct suppliers” was its official position. The cotton row has spilled over into the entertainment world, with Chinese celebrities dropping several foreign retail labels, including six U.S. brands such as Nike.
 New Balance, Under Armour, Tommy Hilfiger and Converse, owned by Nike, have come under fire in China for statements saying they would not use Xinjiang cotton.
   “I can confirm that Uniqlo’s Chinese brand ambassadors have terminated their contracts,” said a Fast Retailing spokesperson.
  At least 27 Chinese movie stars and singers have declared in the past two days that they would stop cooperating with foreign brands.
  Their decision was widely praised by Chinese internet users for being patriotic and trended high on the popular Twitter-like microblog Weibo.
 “I have bought these kinds of products in the past and this situation doesn’t mean that I will now throw them away, destroy them or something like that,” said graduate Lucy Liu outside a Beijing shopping mall.
 “What I’ll do is just avoid buying them for the moment.”
 Beyond the fashion and retail industry, China sanctioned British organisations and individuals on Friday over what it called “lies and disinformation” about Xinjiang, days after Britain imposed sanctions of its own.
 “China is firmly determined to safeguard its national sovereignty, security and development interests, and warns the UK side not to go further down the wrong path,” the Chinese Foreign Ministry said. “Otherwise, China will resolutely make further reactions.”
 The sanctions are the latest sign of deteriorating relations between London and Beijing, including China’s crackdown on dissent in the former British colony of Hong Kong, which had been guaranteed its freedoms when it returned to Chinese rule in 1997.

 def rotate3d（角度，顶点）：rftclecles = rotate_x（角度[0]，顶点）rftctions = rotate_y（角度[1]，revcticrics）rftctions = rotate_z（角度[2]，revcticrics）返回refrices def rotate_x（θ，顶点） ：sin_theta =数学。 SIN（THETA）COS_THETA =数学。 COS（θ）rotated_vertices = []用于顶点的v：打印（＆＃34;：＆＃34; v）x = v [0] y = v [1] z = v [2] rx = x ry = y * cos_theta  -  z * sin_theta rz = z * cos_theta + y * sin_theta rotated_vertices。附加（（rx，ry，rz））返回rotated_vertices def rotate_y（θ，顶点）：sin_theta = math。 SIN（THETA）COS_THETA =数学。 COS（θ）rotated_vertices = []对于顶点的v：x = v [0] y = v [1] z = v [2] rx = x * cos_theta + z * sin_theta ry = y rz = z * cos_theta  -  x * sin_theta rotated_vertices。附加（（rx，ry，rz））返回rotated_vertices def rotate_z（theta，顶点）：sin_theta = math。 SIN（THETA）COS_THETA =数学。 cos（theta）rotated_vertices = []对于顶点的v：x = v [0] y = v [1] z = v = v [2] rx = x * cos_theta  -  y * sin_theta ry = y * cos_theta + x * sin_theta rz = z rotated_vertices。附加（（rx，ry，rz））返回rotated_vertices

note_en:
Welcome folks, I hope you are keeping safe and well. I’m going to spendsome time on a series of articles and some tooling to aid in the creationof brushes and technical art for   TiltBrush software.
 This article is intended to be the first in a series. Please be aware thatthe Python code in this article and in the associated toolset is deliberatelywritten to be verbose and expressive. I have taken this approach because Iwant it to be accessible and easily understood. This is not optimal python,nor good style. You have been warned!
   The most fundamental aspect of a  TiltBrush sketch is called a  Control Point.This is essentially a single vertice on a curve in 3-dimensional space witha couple of other properties, such as pressure and timestamp. These propertiescombined are used when rendering a brush stroke. For this project we will firstneed to build a line primitive, which we will later use to build other morecomplex primitives.
 (If you would like to read more information about the TiltBrush file format, Ihave written about it before  here).
  A  stroke in a TiltBrush sketch is a series of ordered  Control Points.We can represent this stroke as a 3 dimensional line in our code. We willneed to make sure that the vertices on the line are equidistant, not spaced toofar apart, and in the correct order with no duplicates.
 This is easy enough to achieve with a recursive function that finds the midpointof a line segment until the line segment length is short enough and then exitsup through the recursion. As a refresher, the midpoint and segment length formulasin 2d and 3d are.
  $$d = \sqrt{(x2 -x1)^2 + (y2 - y1)^2}$$ $$d = \sqrt{(x2 -x1)^2 + (y2 - y1)^2 + (z2 - z1)^2}$$  $$(x,y) = \left(\dfrac{x1+x2}2,\dfrac{y1+y2}2\right)$$ $$(x,y,z) = \left(\dfrac{x1+x2}2,\dfrac{y1+y2}2,\dfrac{z1+z2}2\right)$$ Most of the code to solve this is mundane, but I did like the recursivemethod of subdividing the line segments. When I am finished this experimentalproject and series of blog articles I’ll share  all of the code and toolswith you.
 def  __subdivide ( self ,  segment ):  seg_len  =  self . __length ( segment )  if  seg_len  <  self . max_seg_size :  self . ctrl_pts . append ( segment )  return  segments  =  self . __split_segment ( segment )  for  s  in  segments :  self . __subdivide ( s )
 Once these operations are complete we have a nice subdivided line segmentwhich can be used to render a stroke inside of  TiltBrush.
   Although it is not necessary I think it is very convenient to have polyhedratransformations based on the  centroid or  center mass of the polyhedra.This makes it much easier to postion shapes based on world coordinates.
 It took a while to find good solutions that derive the centroid of polyhedra. Ifound two recommended solutions, both apply to non-self-intersecting closed3D polyhedron but obtain two different properties. I’m going to stand on theshoulders of giants, and convert these solutions to code.
 The first approach uses an average of the vertice vectors in a mesh to obtain thecentroid of the  vertices – the second uses a  divergence theorem to obtain thecenter-mass (and area) of the polyhedra. Both properties are very useful for differenttypes of meshes ( 3d line vs 3d mesh for example)
 To aid in computing the above I created a  vector class for 3 dimensions, andadded some utility methods to it for calculating dot, and cross products – as wellas magnitude and direction. I also added some functionality to facilitate vectorarithmetic. This class can be found in the reference section of this article.
   The centroid of the vertices is the point derived from the arithmetic meanof all of the vertices in the polyhedra. This is the case for  2,  3,and indeed  n-dimensions.
  To obtain the centroid of a list of vertices , first organize the vertice pointdata as follows:
$$P =\begin{bmatrix}x_1 & x_2 & x_3 & \dots &x_n\\y_1 & y_2 & y_3 & \dots &y_n\\z_1 & z_2 & z_3 & \dots &z_n\\\end{bmatrix}$$ In our code, this is a pretty simple operation, but it makes thecentroid calculation easier in the next step.
 rows  =  3  # x, y, z  cols  =  len ( PTS )  # n  P  =  zeros_matrix ( rows ,  cols )  # populate the matrix  for  i  in  range ( rows ):  for  j  in  range ( cols ):  P [ i ][ j ]  =  PTS [ j ][ i ]
 And now to calculate the centroid  C, we just iterate over the matrix  P andobtain the arithmetic average.
$$C=\begin{bmatrix}x\\y\\z\end{bmatrix}=\frac 1 n\Biggl(\begin{bmatrix}x_1\\y_1\\z_1\end{bmatrix}+\begin{bmatrix}x_2\\y_2\\z_2\end{bmatrix}+\begin{bmatrix}x_3\\y_3\\z_3\end{bmatrix}+\dots+\begin{bmatrix}x_n\\y_n\\z_n\end{bmatrix}\Biggl)$$  lst  =  []  for  i  in  range ( cols ):  _m  =  zeros_matrix ( 1 ,  3 )  _m [ 0 ][ 0 ]  =  P [ 0 ][ i ]  _m [ 0 ][ 1 ]  =  P [ 1 ][ i ]  _m [ 0 ][ 2 ]  =  P [ 2 ][ i ]  lst . append ( _m )  _msum  =  add_matrices ( lst )  sc  =  1  /  cols  centroid_matrix  =  multiply_matrix_by_scalar ( _msum ,  sc )
   Calculating the centroid using the triangles (faces) of a polyhedra is more involved.Actually,  surprisingly more involved. It requires some intricate (for me) calculus for ageneral solution.
 It took me a  long time to understand this approach as my calculus is rusty. To be frankI  still do not have a firm intuition for this. I will try to revisit this in a future articleas it is extremely interesting to me. I do understand it from the application perspective soI am going to forge forward. Just be comforted, that if you find this math difficult to grok,you are not alone.
 The process is described precisely  here, I willinclude the formula and my translation of it to python code.
 In this approach  Ai (i=0 -> N-1) is the count of triangular faces on the polyhedron  P.So  Ai is a triangle. For each of these triangles  i– it is assumed that the vertices of thetriangle  ai, bi, ci are ordered counter clockwise. We can calculate the  outer unit-normal n for each triangle of  P as.
$$n_i= \hat{n_i} /|\hat{n_i}|, \enspace where \enspace \hat{n_i} = (b_i−a_i)⊗(c_i−a_i)$$ The little ^ symbol means unit vector, the ⊗ is the product measure. From this, we cancalculate the volume  V of  P using the  divergence theoremthis is because  x · nᵢ is a constant on each triangle and the area of each triangle is:
  The theorem has the following form, the sigma is sort of like a  for loop. The long S typesymbol is a definite integral which can be thought of as a sort of sum function – these functionsare used a lot in calculus to derive areas – specifically areas under curves. There is a good introto integral calculus  here
$$V = \int_{P}\enspace 1 = \cfrac{1}{3}\int_{\partial P}\enspace x \cdot n = \cfrac{1}{3}\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}\int_{A_i} \enspace a_i \cdot n_i = \cfrac{1}{6}\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}a_i\cdot \hat{n_i}$$ So the centroid of  P will be in set of real numbers in 3 dimensions  R3. Where  C is centroid,and  V is volume:
$$C = \cfrac{1}{V} \enspace \int_{P}x$$ We now apply the divergence theorem again, where the  standard basis is denoted as  e1, e2, e3, allowing us to obtain the three coordinates of the centroid:
$$c \cdot e_d = \frac{1}{V} \enspace \int_{\partial P} \frac{1}{2} (x \cdot e_d)^2(x \cdot e_d) = \frac{1}{2V}\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \int_{A_i} (x \cdot e_d)^2(n_i \cdot e_d) \enspace d=1,2,3$$ $$\int_{A_i} (x \cdot e_d)^2(n_i \cdot e_d) \\\\= \frac{1}{6} \hat{n_i} \cdot e_d([\frac{1}{2}(a_i+b_i)\cdot e_d]^2 + [\frac{1}{2}(b_i+c_i)\cdot e_d]^2 + [\frac{1}{2}(c_i+a_i)\cdot e_d]^2)\\\\= \frac{1}{24} \hat{n_i} \cdot e_d([(a_i + b_i) \cdot e_d]^2 + [(b_i + c_i) \cdot e_d]^2 + [(c_i + a_i) \cdot e_d]^2)$$ I do not fully understand how the above relates to the standard midpoint sampling quadratureformula for triangles, that remains to be explored.
 This is the translation to code,  triangle is a custom class that I created to represent atriangle – properties  a,  b, and  c are  vector3 objects – remember my vector3 classis in the reference section).  triangles is an ordered list of triangle objects obtained fromparsing a mesh.
 total_volume  =  0.0  centroid_integral  =  Vec3 . zero ()  # for each triangle in an ordered list of triangles that make up the mesh  for  triangle  in  triangles :  b_a  =  triangle . b  -  triangle . a  c_a  =  triangle . c  -  triangle . a  # compute area-magnitude normal for this triangle and add it to the   # total volume of this polyhedra  n  =  b_a . cross ( c_a )  total_volume  +=  triangle . a . dot ( n )  /  6.0  # compute and then add the current triangle contribution to the overall   # centroid integral for each dimension.  # X  x_contribution  =  n . x  *  math . pow (( triangle . a . x  +  triangle . b . x ),  2 )  +  math . pow (( triangle . b . x  +  triangle . c . x ),  2 )  +  math . pow (( triangle . c . x  +  triangle . a . x ),  2 )  centroid_integral . x  =  centroid_integral . x  +  x_contribution  # Y  y_contribution  =  n . y  *  math . pow (( triangle . a . y  +  triangle . b . y ),  2 )  +  math . pow (( triangle . b . y  +  triangle . c . y ),  2 )  +  math . pow (( triangle . c . y  +  triangle . a . y ),  2 )  centroid_integral . y  =  centroid_integral . y  +  y_contribution  # Z  z_contribution  =  n . z  *  math . pow (( triangle . a . z  +  triangle . b . z ),  2 )  +  math . pow (( triangle . b . z  +  triangle . c . z ),  2 )  +  math . pow (( triangle . c . z  +  triangle . a . z ),  2 )  centroid_integral . z  =  centroid_integral . z  +  z_contribution  # finally scale each centroid dimension by the inverse volume  x_scaled  =  centroid_integral . x  *  ( 1.0  /  ( 24.0  *  2.0  *  total_volume ))  y_scaled  =  centroid_integral . y  *  ( 1.0  /  ( 24.0  *  2.0  *  total_volume ))  z_scaled  =  centroid_integral . z  *  ( 1.0  /  ( 24.0  *  2.0  *  total_volume ))  # and return the centroid!  return  Vec3 ( x_scaled ,  y_scaled ,  z_scaled )
  We can now apply the above theory and code to derive the centroid of a mesh. I found amesh pumpkin online which is a pretty nice test. It is low-poly, and it has an unusualshape. It is also an enclosed polyhedra that does not self-intersect. Here arethe results, the red point is the centroid.
  That was fun! I didn’t anticipate this complexity, so it turned into a really neat littleadventure. Now lets move onwards! Lets get this picture printed!
  To generate art using our work above, we will need to create some shape primitives. Butbefore we do that, we also need to know how to rotate, and position these primitivesotherwise it would be a very boring picture consisting of all pixels overlapped in a singleposition 😂
 In the future we will discuss and add functionality to scale, and also skew primitives too!
  The translation operation should displace all of the points in a geometric object by afixed distance in a given direction. The formula for a translation is:
$$P' = P + d$$ For all points  P on the object. This translates (🥁) to the following python code.We use our vertice centroid solution that we discussed earlier.
 def  translate_by_centroid3d ( centroid ,  delta ,  vertices ):  # translate centroid   translated_centroid  =  translate_vertice3d ( centroid ,  delta )  dv  =  ( translated_centroid [ 0 ]  -  centroid [ 0 ],  translated_centroid [ 1 ]  -  centroid [ 1 ],  translated_centroid [ 2 ]  -  centroid [ 2 ])  translated_vertices  =  []  for  v  in  vertices :  x  =  v [ 0 ]  y  =  v [ 1 ]  z  =  v [ 2 ]  tx  =  x  +  dv [ 0 ]  ty  =  y  +  dv [ 1 ]  tz  =  z  +  dv [ 2 ]  translated_vertices . append (( tx ,  ty ,  tz ))  return  ( translated_centroid ,  translated_vertices )  def  translate_vertice3d ( vertice ,  delta ):  x  =  vertice [ 0 ]  y  =  vertice [ 1 ]  z  =  vertice [ 2 ]  tx  =  x  +  delta [ 0 ]  ty  =  y  +  delta [ 1 ]  tz  =  z  +  delta [ 2 ]  return  ( tx ,  ty ,  tz )
  A rotation operation is a little more involved, we want to rotate a 3d shapearound its centroid. The transformation matrix for this operation is:
$$Rx = Rx(\theta) =\begin{bmatrix}1 & 0 & 0 \\0 & \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\0 & \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}\\\\\enspace\\\\Ry = Ry(\theta) =\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\0 & 1 & 0 \\-\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}\\\\\enspace\\\\Rz = Rz(\theta) =\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ We can create the following code that mostly emulates the above transformation.But we will approach scale and skew in another post. For now, this is my naivesolution:
 def  rotate3d ( angle ,  vertices ):  rvertices  =  rotate_x ( angle [ 0 ], vertices )  rvertices  =  rotate_y ( angle [ 1 ], rvertices )  rvertices  =  rotate_z ( angle [ 2 ], rvertices )  return  rvertices  def  rotate_x ( theta ,  vertices ):  sin_theta  =  math . sin ( theta )  cos_theta  =  math . cos ( theta )  rotated_vertices  =  []  for  v  in  vertices :  print ( ":" , v )  x  =  v [ 0 ]  y  =  v [ 1 ]  z  =  v [ 2 ]  rx  =  x  ry  =  y  *  cos_theta  -  z  *  sin_theta  rz  =  z  *  cos_theta  +  y  *  sin_theta  rotated_vertices . append (( rx ,  ry ,  rz ))  return  rotated_vertices def  rotate_y ( theta ,  vertices ):  sin_theta  =  math . sin ( theta )  cos_theta  =  math . cos ( theta )  rotated_vertices  =  []  for  v  in  vertices :  x  =  v [ 0 ]  y  =  v [ 1 ]  z  =  v [ 2 ]  rx  =  x  *  cos_theta  +  z  *  sin_theta  ry  =  y  rz  =  z  *  cos_theta  -  x  *  sin_theta  rotated_vertices . append (( rx ,  ry ,  rz ))  return  rotated_vertices def  rotate_z ( theta ,  vertices ):  sin_theta  =  math . sin ( theta )  cos_theta  =  math . cos ( theta )  rotated_vertices  =  []  for  v  in  vertices :  x  =  v [ 0 ]  y  =  v [ 1 ]  z  =  v [ 2 ]  rx  =  x  *  cos_theta  -  y  *  sin_theta  ry  =  y  *  cos_theta  +  x  *  sin_theta  rz  =  z  rotated_vertices . append (( rx ,  ry ,  rz ))  return  rotated_vertices
  To make this article a reasonable size, we will focus on a two simple primitivesfirst then in future articles we will get more advanced. For now we will make  pixelsand  voxels! I think we all know what pixels are, but a voxel is a cuboid, its apixel for 3-dimensions.
  I created a pixel as a square that consisted of four lines in Tiltbrush, and then twolines that form an  X as the fill for the pixel. I have included the code below:
 class  TBPixel :  """  This class represents a pixel in a tiltbrush drawing. It   contains an array of strokes used to draw the cuboid.   3-------2   | |  | |  | |  0-------1   """  def  __init__ ( self ,  position ,  color ,  width ,  height ):  # w, h  assert_valid_vec3_type ( position )  assert_valid_float_type ( width )  assert_valid_float_type ( height )  assert_valid_tb_color_type ( color )  self . __plane  =  Plane ( position ,  width ,  height )  self . __color  =  color  self . __strokes_filled  =  ()  self . __strokes_unfilled  =  ()  self . __generate_strokes ()  @property  def  position ( self ):  return  self . __plane . position  @position.setter  def  position ( self ,  position ):  assert_valid_vec3_type ( position )  self . __plane . position  =  position  self . __generate_strokes ()  @property  def  strokes_filled ( self ):  return  self . __strokes_filled  @property  def  strokes_unfilled ( self ):  return  self . __strokes_unfilled  @property  def  color ( self ):  return  self . __color  @property  def  plane ( self ):  return  self . __plane  def  __generate_strokes ( self ):  # line from 0 -> 1  s0  =  TBLine ( self . plane . pt0 ,  self . plane . pt1 )  # line from 1 -> 2  s1  =  TBLine ( self . plane . pt1 ,  self . plane . pt2 )  # line from 2 -> 3  s2  =  TBLine ( self . plane . pt2 ,  self . plane . pt3 )  # line from 3 -> 0  s3  =  TBLine ( self . plane . pt3 ,  self . plane . pt0 )  self . __strokes_unfilled  =  ( s0 ,  s1 ,  s2 ,  s3 )  # now make the X in the middle  # line from 0 -> 2   sx1  =  TBLine ( self . plane . pt0 ,  self . plane . pt2 )  # line from 1 -> 3  sx2  =  TBLine ( self . plane . pt1 ,  self . plane . pt3 )  self . __strokes_filled  =  ( s0 ,  s1 ,  s2 ,  s3 ,  sx1 ,  sx2 )
   For the voxels, the approach is similar to the pixel above, only we create6 faces (6 pixels) that make up a cube shape. I have included the codebelow:
 class  TBCuboid :  """  This class represents a cuboid in a tiltbrush drawing. It   contains an array of strokes used to draw the cuboid.   7-------6 (max)  /| /|  4-+-----5 |   | | | | y (+)  | 3-----+-2 |   |/ |/ |/  (min) 0-------1 --+-----x (+)  /   z (+)   Front-Back or Back-Front Z-Axis  """  def  __init__ ( self ,  position ,  color ,  size = Vec3 ( 1.0 ,  1.0 ,  1.0 )):  # w, h, d  assert_valid_vec3_type ( position )  assert_valid_vec3_type ( size )  assert_valid_tb_color_type ( color )  self . __cuboid  =  Cuboid ( position ,  size )  self . __color  =  color  self . __generate_strokes_unfilled ()  @property  def  faces ( self ):  return  ( self . __face1 ,  self . __face2 ,  self . __face3 ,  self . __face4 ,  self . __face5 ,  self . __face6 )  @property  def  color ( self ):  return  self . __color  @property  def  cuboid ( self ):  return  self . __cuboid  def  __generate_strokes_unfilled ( self ):  # left face stroke  lf_sl_0_3  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt0 ,  self . cuboid . pt3 )  lf_sl_3_7  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt3 ,  self . cuboid . pt7 )  lf_sl_7_4  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt7 ,  self . cuboid . pt4 )  lf_sl_4_0  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt4 ,  self . cuboid . pt0 )  self . __face1  =  TBFace ( lf_sl_0_3 ,  lf_sl_3_7 ,  lf_sl_7_4 ,  lf_sl_4_0 )  # back face stroke  bf_sl_3_2  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt3 ,  self . cuboid . pt2 )  bf_sl_2_6  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt2 ,  self . cuboid . pt6 )  bf_sl_6_7  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt6 ,  self . cuboid . pt7 )  bf_sl_7_3  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt7 ,  self . cuboid . pt3 )  self . __face2  =  TBFace ( bf_sl_3_2 ,  bf_sl_2_6 ,  bf_sl_6_7 ,  bf_sl_7_3 )  # right face stroke  rf_sl_1_2  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt1 ,  self . cuboid . pt2 )  rf_sl_2_6  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt2 ,  self . cuboid . pt6 )  rf_sl_6_5  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt6 ,  self . cuboid . pt5 )  rf_sl_5_1  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt5 ,  self . cuboid . pt1 )  self . __face3  =  TBFace ( rf_sl_1_2 ,  rf_sl_2_6 ,  rf_sl_6_5 ,  rf_sl_5_1 )  # front face stroke  ff_sl_0_1  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt0 ,  self . cuboid . pt1 )  ff_sl_1_5  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt1 ,  self . cuboid . pt5 )  ff_sl_5_4  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt5 ,  self . cuboid . pt4 )  ff_sl_4_0  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt4 ,  self . cuboid . pt0 )  self . __face4  =  TBFace ( ff_sl_0_1 ,  ff_sl_1_5 ,  ff_sl_5_4 ,  ff_sl_4_0 )  # top face stroke  tf_sl_4_5  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt4 ,  self . cuboid . pt5 )  tf_sl_5_6  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt5 ,  self . cuboid . pt6 )  tf_sl_6_7  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt6 ,  self . cuboid . pt7 )  tf_sl_7_4  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt7 ,  self . cuboid . pt4 )  self . __face5  =  TBFace ( tf_sl_4_5 ,  tf_sl_5_6 ,  tf_sl_6_7 ,  tf_sl_7_4 )  # bottom face stroke  bf_sl_0_1  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt0 ,  self . cuboid . pt1 )  bf_sl_1_2  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt1 ,  self . cuboid . pt2 )  bf_sl_2_3  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt2 ,  self . cuboid . pt3 )  bf_sl_3_0  =  TBLine ( self . cuboid . pt3 ,  self . cuboid . pt0 )  self . __face6  =  TBFace ( bf_sl_0_1 ,  bf_sl_1_2 ,  bf_sl_2_3 ,  bf_sl_3_0 )

title: 在虚拟现实中打印

note:
当谈到最有用的东西时，我们直观地知道他们的结构在前面 - 或引起他们的功能。例如，您的计算机具有一个结构，使您可以阅读本文。
 金钱也有一种结构，可以实现某些功能，但是，当被要求描述它时，许多人将描述多少钱，而不是描述其结构。我将展示为什么这是如此，为什么它是迷失方向迷惑的主要来源，深深妨碍了我们理解我们的世界的能力。
   让我使用思想实验来说明当功能和结构混合时会发生什么。想象一个带有里面的东西的盒子。你的朋友说'这是一个椅子'......
  听到这一点后，你的大脑会让人的椅子上的结构形象，并且该图像具有直观的功能概念。结构形象可能因人的人而异，但它可能包括在坚固的垂直腿上设定的坚固的水平方形表面包括垂直靠背。功能定义可能是“坐在的东西”，而且驻扎在高架架子上的东西'。
 然而，想象一下，你的朋友打开了盒子，揭示了你期待的一个不同的结构......
   他而不是坚实的结构，揭示了由塑料制成的弹性，柔软，球形的结构，充满空气。这是通常与“运动球”一词（有时称为瑜伽球）（有时称为瑜伽球）的结构图像，这个球通常用于做AB练习和普拉提，但它也是孩子们反弹的最爱虽然玩。有些人偶尔也坐在他们​​身上。
 一点后来，你在休息室的实际椅子上撒谎，而你的朋友坐在他的球'椅子'。你指向一个高架，说...... 
这里发生了什么？嗯，对于有用物体的几乎所有定义都是结构和功能的综合，它只是通过同时观察我们的意义。然而，具有完全不同的结构的对象可以共享一个狭窄的功能重叠区域。如果你忽略了他们的结构，并专注于他们狭隘的常见功能，虽然也忽略了他们不分享的所有这些功能，但你可以简要断言他们是“同样的事情”。
 这就是上述人类最初被设法打电话给球的人，但它允许他的朋友通过呼吁通常与椅子相关的不同功能来报复，但没有与球相关联。
 大多数人只在开玩笑时执行这个“功能 - 多种结构”混合（例如，一个女人坐在她的爱人身上，说'我有什么性感的椅子'），但如果你坚持认真对待它语言降解。如果我将“椅子”的定义局限于“您可以坐在”的单一功能描述中，那么该术语必须适用于数千 - 偶数数百万的对象。我可以坐在我的车的引擎盖上，我家的屋顶，山顶上的岩石，吉他放大器，甚至是一个懒散的线条。
  通过给出所有这些的东西，他们的单独结构和各种能力都被释放出来。如果发生这种情况，术语“椅子”也会在头脑中产生任何结构形象。相反，你只是看到模糊的空白空间。
 含义是这样：如果你只能描述某事的功能而没有能够描述它的结构，你就在模糊的领域，其中意义可以崩溃。
    所以这是一个运行的测试。问朋友，他们看到他们听到名词'金钱'时看到的结构形象是什么。请他们在这个盒子里画它......
  他们的任务是绘制金钱的结构，这在想象的金钱职能之前。 
我预测，十分之十，这个人将想象的图像主要由货币令牌等货币令牌，银行账户中的数字，应用程序上的数字或其他编号对象，无论是数字还是物理的。
 检查金钱的主导结构图像的一种方法是将术语输入到Google图像搜索中。这里是…
  你在这里看到一个“结构”吗？不，我们在这里看到的是最好被描述为较大结构的表面层的一部分。确实，在世界面前出现的物理资金代币，但它们只能工作，因为它们集成到激活它们的巨大系统中。
 显示现金代币的图像就像显示一个结构的幽灵般的草图（或者也许像装饰金钱的图像应该被绘制的白色矩形的边界）。它是最好的局部表面外观，但这是许多人的形象“金钱结构”停止。
 如果我询问他们如何运作的结构如何运作，会发生什么？嗯，鉴于他们的结构形象由带有数字的脆弱物体组成，他们将面临存在的存在困境，同时尝试与他们每天经历的金钱的事实上的资金进行协调。很容易看一把椅子如何通过看着它的全部结构来阻止你，但不是很容易看金币如何通过看着它结构的片段来掌握经济。
 这是一个非常好的例子，显示有一个具有这种确切存在的困境的人。它在Tiktok上的病毒性，值得一看，因为它表明他们努力使表面级别的物体感到很小，除非在更大的结构中看到。鉴于他们无法找到对象本身的对象的力量的解释，他们得出结论，以某种方式我们必须“弥补”他们的力量，好像金钱代币就像彼得平底锅一样想象力的力量。
 这一思想是居住在我们头脑中的某种虚构的系统 - 而不是作为一个密集的网络结构，字面上吞噬我们 - 非常普遍，并被发现在政治频谱上。就像我想象的那种神秘的东西游泳在一个黑暗的湖面下方游泳，那些金钱结构应该容易填补情绪骚乱，模糊性和炒陈述。这是一个世界，有些人瞥见遥远的中央银行在雾中笼罩着，而其他人则声称“金钱不是真实”，这是“基于信仰”。 
请记住，没有明确结构的功能是一种肯定的方式来产生神秘主义的感觉，这达到了金钱的时候，为什么我们努力看到这个结构？
 第一个原因是显而易见的：它是一个非常大的结构，它的纯粹大小使其对小型人类不可见。我们沉浸在其中，到了我们无法从中获得足够距离的地步。
 然而，与第一个有关的第二个原因。一个人在哪里努力看到金钱的结构转向帮助？人们在哪里去学习金钱？
 许多人认为，要了解金钱，他们应该拿起经济学教科书。我经常听到人们说，'我真的不懂钱，我不是一个经济学家'，仿佛作为经济学家会睁开眼睛向我们周围的货币网站。
 然而，拿起经济学教科书可能是他们可以做的最糟糕的事情，因为使用标准经济学的货币理解就像使用NovoCaine达到启示：它可能有助于沉闷现实的痛苦，但在这个过程中留下你麻木。
 我为什么这么说？嗯，标准经济学教科书专注于将我们的注意力从金钱的结构中汲取，而是通过聚焦它的含糊不清的“金钱的功能”，到许多人似乎相信后者是前者。
 当被要求定义金钱时，那些在大学学习的人常见的是，以引用“金钱”的经济学，是“资金的经济学，是一个价值，交换媒介和账户单位”，好像这些是结构描述货币体系。 
但他们不是。那些是从主要结构中出现的次要性质，并且对它们的固定作用类似于一种盲目。经济学并没有帮助我们看到金钱。它有助于我们忽略它。
 写这篇文章时，我在我的书架上经历了两个经济学教科书来证实这一点。两者都遵循了通过勾选盒练习间接描述货币的常见做法：而不是说“这是金钱的结构。它做了一个，b和c“，他们说”任何事情，b和c是金钱“。这是一种在没有命名的情况下躲避结构的方式，有点像将椅子描述为“任何让你坐在它上的东西，也可以站在它上”。
   给予主流经济学以最好的含糊不清和避难的解释框架，人们要么留下来源，要么通过日常经验来解决这些结构，或者他们被遗弃在youtube等地方搜索它，各种批评者将为“钱如何真正有效'。
 不幸的是，这些所谓的非符合者中的许多不符是因为经济学家所在的功能定义，但是，由于经济学家来说，但是已经部分地瞥见了结构的一些更明显的部分（如中央银行），然后它们融合了后者的稻草人版本与前者。这会创造阴谋陈述，就像“中央银行已经让我们相信金钱，这不是真实的！”
 这只是上述“金钱只是信仰”的想法的变化，这出现了，如果你只看一下金钱结构的表面。阴谋变化只是断言，信仰的来源从一些强大的党派的某种欺骗行为中出现，而不是通过一些社会共识，但两个版本都是通过某种精神诡计举行的金钱，而不是通过实际结构。
 这不会将公众留在一个非常好的位置。首席竞争者填补金钱的空间，应该是没有结构形象的经济学家，以及与稻草人形象的阴谋理论家。您可以从主流否认或非主流曲柄中挑选。
  首先，我们必须开始绘制结构，因此每次发出单词“金钱”这个词时，会出现清晰和完整的结构形象。在本通讯中，我已经开始了这个过程，但我们在完整结构揭示自己之前有很长的路要走 
其次，我们不必完全同意结构的样子，但我们确实需要同意它应该是应该前景的。这将是目前现状向前迈出的重大步骤，这只是拒绝前景
 第三，我们必须能够明确地区分个人的个人经验 - 在街道层面使用金钱令牌的日常感受 - 以及超越这一点的隐藏结构。就像我们经历过太阳作为“升起”而不是在地球转弯时固定的东西的东西，有一种有可能与其结构的现实不同的现象学领域，有时 - 有时 - 模糊的功能定义可以在这个领域得到。然而，当涉及金钱的政治时，它是一个彻头彻尾的致命，留在那个领域
 在尝试设计替代货币系统时，留在该领域也损害了。目前的货币制度有许多伪挑战者，其中许多挑战者利用了金钱的功能定义的模糊性，以逃避将自己归于解决方案。更多的是来。
 在本系列的下一部分中，我将谈谈神奇的桥梁和印第安纳琼斯作为看到结构的一步，所以如果你还没有，请注册！
note_en:
When it comes to most useful things, we intuitively know that their structure precedes - or gives rise to - their functions. For example, your computer has a structure that enables you to read this article.
 Money too has a structure that enables certain functions to exist, but, when asked to describe it, many people will describe what money  does, rather than describing its structure. I’m going to show why this is the case, and why it’s major source of disorientating confusion that deeply hampers our ability to understand our world.
   Let me use a thought-experiment to illustrate what happens when functions and structures get mixed up. Imagine a box with something inside it. Your friend says ‘it’s a chair’…
  Upon hearing this, your brain conjures a  structural image of a chair, and that image comes along with an intuitive notion of its  functions. The structural image might vary from person to person, but it’s probably going to consist of a solid horizontal square surface set upon sturdy vertical legs with a vertical backrest. The functional definitions might be ‘thing for sitting on’, and ‘thing for standing on to reach high shelf’.
 Imagine, however, your friend opens the box and reveals a different structure to the one you were expecting…
   Rather than a solid construction, he reveals a bouncy, soft, spherical structure made of plastic and filled with air. This is the structural image that normally goes along with the word ‘exercise ball’ (sometimes called Yoga ball, or Pilates ball), and this ball is normally used for doing ab exercises and Pilates, but it’s also a favourite for kids to bounce on whilst playing. Some people also sit on them occasionally.
 A bit later you’re slouching on an actual chair in the lounge while your friend sits on his ball ‘chair’. You point to a high shelf and say…
  What’s going on here? Well, almost all of our definitions for useful objects are a  composite of both the structural and functional, and it’s only through viewing  both simultaneously that we make sense of a word. Nevertheless, objects with entirely different structures can share a narrow zone of  functional overlap. If you  ignore their structures, and focus solely upon their narrow common function, whilst also ignoring all those functions they do not share, you can briefly assert that they are ‘the same thing’.
 This is how the man above originally managed to call a ball ‘a chair’, but it allows his friend to get revenge by calling upon a different function normally associated with a chair, but not associated with a ball.
 Most people only do this ‘function-to-multiple-structure’ blending when joking (for example, a woman sits on her lover and says, ‘what a sexy chair I have’), but if you insist on doing it seriously it causes language to degrade. If I confined the definition of ‘chair’ to the single functional description of ‘thing you can sit on’ then the term would have to be applied to thousands - even millions - of objects. I can sit on the hood of my car, the roof of my house, a rock on top of a mountain, a guitar amplifier, and even a slackline.
  By giving all of these things the same noun, their separate structures and diverse capacities are invisibilised. If this happened, the term ‘chair’ would also generate no structural image in your head. Rather, you’d just see vague blank space.
 The implications is this:  if you can only describe something’s function without being able to describe its structure, you’re in a realm of vagueness where meaning can collapse.
    So here’s a test to run. Ask a friend what structural image they see when they hear the noun ‘money’. Ask them to draw it in this box…
  Their task is to draw the structure of money, which  precedes the imagined functions of money.
 I predict that, nine times out of ten, the image the person will imagine consists mostly of monetary artefacts like cash tokens, numbers in bank accounts, numbers on apps, or other numbered objects, whether digital or physical.
 One way to check the dominant structural image of money is to type the term into a Google image search. Here it is…
  Do you see a ‘structure’ here? No. What we are seeing here is best described as  one part of the surface layer of a much larger structure. It is true that there are physical money tokens that appear before us in the world, but they only work because they’re integrated into a huge system that activates them.
 Showing images of cash tokens is like showing a ghostly sketch of 5% of a structure (or perhaps like decorating the border of the white rectangle where the image of money was supposed to be drawn). It is at best a partial surface appearance, but this is where many people’s image of the ‘structure of money’ stops.
 What happens if I then ask them how this imagined structure works? Well, given that their structural image consists of flimsy objects with numbers, they’re going to face an existential dilemma whilst trying to reconcile that with the  de facto power of money they experience on a daily basis. It’s easy to see how a chair holds you up by looking at its full structure, but not very easy to see how money holds an economy up by looking at a fragment of it structure.
 Here’s a very good example  showing someone having this exact existential dilemma. It went viral on TikTok, and it’s worth watching because it shows them struggling to make sense of surface-level objects that make little sense unless seen within a much bigger structure. Given that they are not able to find an explanation for the power of the objects in the objects themselves, they come to the conclusion that somehow we must have ‘made up’ their power, as if money tokens were like Peter Pans held up through the power of imagination.
 This idea of money being some kind of imaginary system residing in our heads - rather than being a dense network structure that literally engulfs us - is extremely common, and is found across the political spectrum. Much like I might imagine all manner of mysterious things swimming below the surface of a dark lake, the blank space where the structure of money should be is easy to fill up with emotional tumult, vagueness, and scrambled statements. It’s a world in which some people glimpse distant central banks shrouded in mist, whilst others assert that ‘money isn’t real’, and that it is ‘based on belief’.
 Remember that functions without clear structures are a sure-fire way to generate a feeling of mysticism, and this abounds when it comes to money, so why is it that we struggle to see the structure?
 The first reason is obvious: it is a  very large structure, and its sheer size renders it invisible to a small-scale human being. We are immersed in it to the point where we cannot get enough distance from it to see it.
 There is, however, a second reason, related to the first. Where does a person who is struggling to see the structure of money turn to for help? Where do people go to learn about money?
 Many people believe that to learn about money they should pick up an economics textbook. I often hear people saying, ‘I don’t really understand money, I’m not an economist’, as if being an economist would open their eyes to the monetary web around us.
 Picking up an economics textbook, however, is probably the worst thing they can do, because using standard economics for monetary understanding is like using novocaine to reach enlightement: it might help dull the pain of reality, but leave you numb to it in the process.
 Why do I say this? Well, standard economics textbooks specialise in drawing our attention  away from the  structure of money, by focusing it instead upon vague ‘functions of money’, to the point where many people seem to believe that the latter is the former.
 When asked for a definition of money, it is common for those who have studied at universities to quote the economics mantra of ‘money is a store of value, medium of exchange, and unit of account’, as if these were structural descriptions of the monetary system.
 But they’re not. Those are secondary properties that emerge from a primary structure, and the fixation upon them acts like a form of blindness. Economics does not help us to see money. It helps us to ignore it.
 When writing this piece, I went through two economics textbooks on my bookshelf to confirm this. Both followed the very common practice of describing money  indirectly through a tick-box exercise: rather than saying “ here is the structure of money. It does A, B, and C”, they say “anything that does A, B and C is money”. It is a way of eluding to the structure without ever naming it, a bit like describing a chair as ‘anything that enables you to sit on it, and also to stand on it’.
   Give that mainstream economics offers at best a vague and evasive explanatory framework for money, people are either left to work out the structure through everyday experience, or they are left to search for it in places like YouTube, where various critics will offer explanations for ‘how money really works’.
 Unfortunately, many of these supposed non-conformists are just as blinded by functional definitions as economists are, but, having partially glimpsed some of the more obvious parts of the structure (like central banks), they then fuse a straw man version of the latter with the former. This creates conspiratorial statements, like ‘central banks have conned us into believing in money which isn’t real!’
 This is just a variation on the aforementioned ‘money is just belief’ idea, which emerges if you only look at the surface of the structure of money. The conspiracy variation just asserts that the source of the belief emerges from some act of deceit by a powerful party, rather than by some social consensus, but both versions are united in presenting money as being held up through some kind of mental trickery, rather than by an actual structure.
 This doesn’t leave the public in a very good position. The chief contenders to fill in the blank space where the stucture of money should be are economists with no structural images, and conspiracy theorists with straw man images. You get to pick from mainstream denialism or non-mainstream crankery.
  Firstly, we must start to draw the structure, so that every time the word ‘money’ is uttered, a clear and full structural image appears. In this newsletter I have already begun that process, but we have a long way to go before the full structure reveals itself
 Secondly, we need not agree exactly on what the structure looks like, but we do need to agree that it  should be foregrounded. This will be a major step forward from the current status quo, which simply refuses to foreground it
 Thirdly, we must be able to make a clear distinction between the individual experience of money - the everyday feeling of using money tokens at a street level - and the hidden structure which transcends that. Much like we experience the sun as a thing that ‘rises’ rather than something that stays fixed while the earth turns, there is a  phenomenological realm of money which can differ from the reality of its structure, and - sometimes - the vague functional definitions can get by in this realm. When it comes to the politics of money, however, it is a downright deadly to stay in that realm
 It is also damaging to stay in that realm when attempting to design alternative money systems. There are many pseudo-challengers to the current monetary regime, many of which exploit the vagueness of functional definitions of money to get away with presenting themselves as solutions. More on this to come.
 In the next part of this series, I’m going to talk about magical bridges and Indiana Jones as a step towards seeing the structure, so sign up if you haven’t already!
title: “金钱的功能”如何将我们视为金钱的结构
title_en: How the 'Functions of Money' Blind Us to the Structure of Money

note:
超过20年，每当我教欧洲历史时，我一直告诉同一个故事给学生。在14世纪的某些时间，盐酸盐菌在中国西部的啮齿动物人口中搬出了令人遗憾的是，对人类疯狂传染性和致命。这种细菌导致黑人死亡，这是一个瘟疫大流行，在几十年中，从亚洲移动到欧洲，无论何处触及所有人类生活的三分之一到一半。虽然瘟疫大流行肯定发生了，但是，我一直在教导细菌的何时何地教学，并且历史尽可能地是不完整的。
    12月，历史学家莫妮卡绿色发表了一个地标文章，这是美国历史评论的四个黑人死亡，可以重写我们对这种残酷和变革大流行的叙述。在其中，她确定了一个“大爆炸”，它创造了四个不同的遗传谱系，这些谱系在世界各地分开蔓延，并发现具体证据证明瘟疫已经从中国传播到1200岁的中亚。这次发现将黑人死亡的起源推回到了一百年多年来，这意味着瘟疫的第一波浪潮不是长期恐怖的爆炸，但九十年内悄悄蔓延到它到达之前危机点。
  随着世界卷曲在其自身全球大流行的队伍下，了解人类今天和整个物种的相对较短的历史中，了解人们如何与大自然相互作用的重要性变得更加重要。绿色告诉我，像瘟疫一样的疾病和可以说是SARS-COV-2（在2019年底转移到人类的人类引起Covid-19之前）不是人类疾病，因为有机体并不依赖于人类宿主进行繁殖（与人类不同适应疟疾或结核病）。它们是动物疾病或动物疾病，但人类仍然是从一个部位到另一个遗址的细菌的载体和运输障碍，使地方性动物疾病变成致命的人类。
  当莫妮卡绿色告诉我时，黑人死亡，是“人们了解欧洲中世纪的几件事之一”。对于学者来说，快速的14世纪的故事包含了绿色叫“黑洞”。当她在20世纪80年代开始职业生涯时，我们并不真正知道“当它发生时，它发生了如何，[或]来自哪里！”现在我们有一个更清晰的图片。
  “黑人死亡和其他前现代瘟疫爆发是每个人在学校学到的东西，或者以梅蒂的Python-esque的方式开玩笑。 IT不是一些大多数公众都认为与现代性或自己的生活特别相关的东西，“美国中世纪院校执行董事Lisa Fagin Davis说。但现在，“随着Covid-19大流行的开始，突然中世纪瘟疫与各地的每个人都有相关。”
  在绿色文章中持久的项目多年来展开。她说，第一次迈出了对瘟疫的已知受害者的古生分析，包括2011年批判性研究。古生物是对保存有机物质的研究 - 真的是身体的任何部分或微生物组，下降到DNA的长死亡生物。这意味着，如果你能找到身体，或最好是很多身体，那你肯定会在黑色死亡中死亡，你常常可以进入杀死它们的特定疾病的DNA并将其与现代和其他前面进行比较 - 现有的菌株。
  这已经以多种方式得到了报酬。首先，由于科学家映射了基因组，他们首先要休息长期挥之不去，疑虑Y. Pestis在黑色死亡中发挥的痛苦（存在普遍但未取消的猜测，其他疾病是错误的）。科学家映射了细菌的基因组，并开始建立一个数据集，透露它随着时间的推移如何发展。绿色在2012年在伦敦，正如伦敦瘟疫公墓的调查结果，毫无疑问地证实了细菌的身份和6月1348年6月伦敦瘟疫的特定遗传谱系。“伦敦的黑死军公墓是特别的因为它被创造成容纳黑人死亡的尸体，“她说，”然后当[瘟疫波]通过时，他们关闭了墓地。我们有文书工作！“ 
绿色成立了自己作为中世纪女性医疗保健的最重要专家，并在被称为跑步的医疗作业上的工作。她仔细分析了稿件传统，揭示了一些文本是归因于意大利南部的妇女托罗塔。但是，其他部分揭示了男性医生试图接管妇女健康市场。这是一个非凡的文本，这对于她的黑色死亡项目而言，不仅通过将她沉浸在医学史上，而且的方法论也是如此。她的纪律，关于文本的发展随着时间的推移，需要将手稿互相进行比较，从父母或原稿中互相建立文本的STOMMA或文本的家谱。她告诉我，这正是一种需要读取突变细菌的系统发育树的技能，以追踪疾病的历史。
  尽管如此，在13世纪的亚洲将黑死者放置不仅仅是遗传数据。绿色需要向量，她希望爆发的文本证据。她小心补充说，在历史时刻试图发现疾病时，“没有证据不是没有缺席的证据”。她的第一步是专注于来自蒙古草原的可爱小啮齿动物：土拨鼠。
  蒙古人为肉和皮革捕猎土拨鼠（这是重量轻和防水），他们带来了他们的啮齿动物偏好，因为亚洲很快被征服者进入了1216左右的天山山脉，征服了一个叫做Qara Khitai的人（本身来自中国北方的难民）。在那里，蒙古将遇到携带瘟疫的土拨鼠，这将成为黑人死亡。在这里，细菌突变的“大爆炸”理论提供了允许我们为黑死的新起点的关键证据。 （为了支持这一理论，她的12月文章载有一个16页的附录在Marmots上！）
  系统发育结果足以让绿色推测13世纪的瘟疫起源，但是当它来到传播机制时，她所拥有的只是猜想 - 直到她发现了蒙古围攻末期爆发的描述在1258年的巴格达。绿色很快就注意到她依赖于许多不同语言的专家来完成这项工作，因为它从中国遍历到直布罗陀的岩石，以及从北极圈到撒哈拉以南非洲的北极圈。
  没有人是所有语言的专家。绿色带来的是一种综合观点，从尖端的科学和人文奖学金中吸引了一个叙述，以及认识到她在宣布Akhbār-iMoghōlān或蒙古新闻的新翻译时发现了她发现的意义。该来源于2009年由伊朗历史学家Iraj Afshar发表于2009年，但仅在2018年被翻译成英文作为伊朗的蒙古，由乔治巷。中世纪的伊朗来源是一个混乱的东西，也许是一个没有生存的更有组织的文本的幸存票据。仍然，绿色意识到蒙古围攻的报告持有她一直在寻找的关键证据。当她在她的文章中引用时，蒙古新闻描述了瘟疫如此可怕，“巴格达的人们不能再应对书面和埋葬死者，所以尸体被扔进了底格里斯河。”但更重要的是绿色，蒙古新闻指出了谷物货车的存在，从Qara Khitai的土地上捣碎小米。
  突然，碎片合身在一起。 “我已经在田山山上看到了田山，”她说，当然，土豆蒙古互动可能导致那里的瘟疫，但没有解释长途传播。 “我在我脑海中放在一起的情景是某种溢出的事件。土拨鼠不会围绕着人。他们是野生动物，不会心甘情愿地与人类互动。所以我不得不想出的生物情景是无论如何都必须转移到另一种啮齿动物中。“
  随着田山的粮食供应与巴格达的瘟疫爆发有关，很容易猜测从土拨鼠移动到其他啮齿动物的细菌，那些骑在谷物中的啮齿动物，瘟疫矢量揭示。 “那是我的尤里卡时刻，”她说。 
她在合适的时间在正确的地方放置了正确的细菌菌株，以便在谷物马车列车中感染的啮齿动物揭示了瘟疫的分布手段。
  “在她的职业生涯中，绿色博士使人文主义和科学的方式使得对瘟疫的起源和传播的方式使人文主义和科学结合在一起，”戴维斯从中世纪学院说。 “她与历史学家，遗传学家，古生物学家，考古学家和其他人的合作解开了瘟疫菌株的遗传复杂性。”
  这种跨学科工作在任何时候都对学者来说是重要的，但现在需要特别相关。 “[绿色]曾致力于破坏不精确和简单的瘟疫叙述，并向现出的公众解释了解背景中历史瘟疫的重要性，”添加戴维斯“[她]的声音一直是我们努力理解我们自己的现代意识 - 日瘟疫。“
  绿色也看到了相关性，特别是因为她对瘟疫变异和大流行的研究，就像Covid-19病原体的新变种一样，在世界各地表现出来。她告诉我，由于科迪德，她的工作并没有改变，但紧迫感所做的。 “瘟疫”，绿色说，“是我们最好的”模型生物“，用于研究流行病的历史，因为它的历史现在如此丰富，纪录片和考古记录受到遗传记录的补充。病毒学家在测序和跟踪SARS-COV-2的所有作品中的所有工作都是与追踪yersinia pestis＆＃39;过去的进化和运动来完成的相同工作。 “
  她希望她的学者专注于人工机构，都在历史 - 那些蒙古和他们的旅行车列车 - 现在。黑人死亡的历史讲述了我们参与创造这种大流行的强大故事：这是＆＃39; T母亲自然只是对我们生气，更不用说命运。这是人类活动。“
  现在，世界才会感谢绿色和许多其他人（从各种学科，时间段和世界各地的学者看学者们的长篇学者） - 反映了对黑人死亡的真实历史的处理。接下来，她告诉我，她有一篇文章与中世纪伊斯兰主义的Nahyan Pancy，关于瘟疫爆发的进一步文字证据，以补充蒙古新闻。这些13世纪的许多消息来源以前都知道，但如果你开始假设瘟疫，直到14世纪瘟疫就不会出现瘟疫，你永远找不到它们。
  她想象学者可能在其他地方发现瘟疫，一旦他们开始看。与此同时，为了了解疾病如何移动的危险仍然至关重要，因为我们与自己的大流行搏斗。我问她认为这一切意味着今天的世界仍然有流行病。她回答说，随着令人痛苦的几个世纪 - 展望未来，“我重建的黑人死亡是100％的新兴传染病故事。 ......“新兴”疾病持续了500-600年!!!“
note_en:
For over 20 years, I’ve been telling the same story to students whenever I teach European history. At some point in the 14th century, the bacterium  Yersinia pestis somehow moved out of the rodent population in western China and became wildly infectious and lethal to humans. This bacterium caused the Black Death, a plague pandemic that moved from Asia to Europe in just a few decades, wiping out one-third to one-half of all human life wherever it touched. Although the plague pandemic definitely happened, the story I’ve been teaching about when, where, and the history of the bacterium has apparently been incomplete, at best.
    In December, the historian Monica Green published a landmark article,   The Four Black Deaths, in the  American Historical Review, that rewrites our narrative of this brutal and transformative pandemic. In it, she identifies a “big bang” that created four distinct genetic lineages that spread separately throughout the world and finds concrete evidence that the plague was already spreading from China to central Asia in the 1200s. This discovery pushes the origins of the Black Death back by over a hundred years, meaning that the first wave of the plague was not a decades-long explosion of horror, but a disease that crept across the continents for over a hundred years until it reached a crisis point.
  As the world reels beneath the strains of its own global pandemic, the importance of understanding how humans interact with nature both today and throughout the relatively short history of our species becomes more critical. Green tells me that diseases like the plague and arguably SARS-CoV-2 ( before it transferred into humans in late 2019 causing Covid-19) are not human diseases, because the organism doesn’t rely on human hosts for reproduction (unlike human-adapted malaria or tuberculosis). They are zoonotic, or animal diseases, but humans are still the carriers and transporters of the bacteria from one site to the other, turning an endemic animal disease into a deadly human one.
  The Black Death, as Monica Green tells me, is “one of the few things that people learn about the European Middle Ages.” For scholars, the fast 14th-century story contained what Green calls a “black hole.” When she began her career in the 1980s, we didn’t really know “when it happened, how it happened, [or] where it came from!” Now we have a much clearer picture.
  “The Black Death and other pre-modern plague outbreaks were something everyone learned about in school, or joked about in a Monty Python-esque way. It wasn't something that most of the general public would have considered particularly relevant to modernity or to their own lives,” says Lisa Fagin Davis, executive director of the Medieval Academy of America. But now, “with the onset of the Covid-19 pandemic, suddenly medieval plagues became relevant to everyone everywhere.”
  The project that culminated in Green’s article unfolded over many years. She says that the first step required paleogenetic analysis of known victims of the plague,  including a critical study 2011.  Paleogenetics is the study of preserved organic material—really any part of the body or the microbiome, down to the DNA—of long dead organisms. This means that if you can find a body, or preferably a lot of bodies, that you’re sure died in the Black Death, you can often access the DNA of the specific disease that killed them and compare it to both modern and other pre-modern strains.
  This has paid off in numerous ways. First, as scientists mapped the genome, they first put to rest long lingering doubts about the role  Y. pestis played in the Black Death (there was widespread but unsubstantiated speculation that other diseases were at fault). Scientists mapped the genome of the bacterium and began building a dataset that revealed how it had evolved over time. Green was in London in 2012 just as findings on the London plague cemetery came out confirming without a doubt both the identity of the bacterium and the specific genetic lineage of the plague that hit London in June 1348. “The Black Death cemetery in London is special because it was created to accommodate bodies from the Black Death,” she says, “and then when [the plague wave] passed, they closed the cemetery. We have the paperwork!”
  Green established herself as the foremost expert in medieval women’s healthcare with her work on a medical treatise  known as  The Trotula. Her careful analysis of manuscript traditions revealed that some of the text was attributable to a southern Italian woman, Trota. Other sections, though, revealed male doctors’ attempts to take over the market for women’s health. It’s a remarkable text that prepared Green for her Black Death project not only by immersing her in the history of medicine, but methodologically as well. Her discipline of philology, the study of the development of texts over time, requires comparing manuscripts to each other, building a  stemma, or genealogy of texts, from a parent or original manuscript. She tells me that this is precisely the same skill one needs to read phylogenetic trees of mutating bacteria in order to trace the history of the disease.
  Still, placing the Black Death in 13th-century Asia required more than genetic data. Green needed a vector, and she hoped for textual evidence of an outbreak. She is careful to add that, when trying to find a disease in a historical moment, the “absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.” Her first step was to focus on a cute little rodent from the Mongolian steppe: the marmot.
  Mongols hunted marmots for meat and leather (which was both lightweight and waterproof), and they brought their rodent preferences with them as the soon-to-be conquerors of Asia moved into the Tian Shan mountains around 1216 and conquered a people called the Qara Khitai (themselves refugees from Northern China). There, the Mongols would have encountered marmots who carried the strain of plague that would become the Black Death. Here, the “big bang” theory of bacterial mutation provides key evidence allowing us a new starting point for the Black Death. (To support this theory, her December article contains a 16-page appendix just on marmots!)
  The phylogenetic findings were enough for Green to speculate about a 13th-century origin for the plague, but when it came to the mechanism of spread, all she had was conjecture—until she found a description of an outbreak at the end of the Mongol siege of Baghdad in 1258. Green is quick to note that she has relied on experts in many different languages to do this work, unsurprisingly since it traverses from China to the rock of Gibraltar, and from near the Arctic Circle to sub-Saharan Africa.
  No one is expert in all the languages. What Green brought was a synthetic view that drew a narrative out of cutting-edge science and humanistic scholarship and the ability to recognize the significance of what she found when she opened a new translation of the  Akhbār-i Moghūlān, or  Mongol News. This source was published for the first time in 2009 by the  Iranian historian Iraj Afshar, but  only translated into English in 2018 as  The Mongols in Iran, by George Lane. The medieval Iranian source is something of a jumble, perhaps the surviving notes for a more organized text that didn’t survive. Still, the report on the Mongol siege, Green realized, held the key piece of evidence she’d been looking for. As she cites in her article,  Mongol News describes pestilence so terrible that the “people of Baghdad could no longer cope with  ablutions and burial of the dead, so bodies were thrown into the Tigris River.” But even more importantly for Green,  Mongol News notes the presence of grain wagons, pounded millet, from the lands of the Qara Khitai.
  Suddenly, the pieces fit together. “I’ve already got my eye on the Tian Shan mountains, where the marmots are,” she says, and of course marmot-Mongol interaction could cause plague there, but didn’t explain long-distance transmission. “The scenario I’m putting together in my head is some sort of spillover event. Marmots don’t hang around people. They’re wild animals that will not willingly interact with humans. So the biological scenario I had to come up with is whatever is in the marmots had to be transferred to another kind of rodent.”
  With the grain supply from Tian Shan linked to plague outbreak in Baghdad, it’s easy to conjecture a bacterium moving from marmots to other rodents, those rodents riding along in grain, and the plague vector revealed. “That was my eureka moment,” she says.
  She had put the correct strain of the bacteria at the right place at the right time so that one infected rodent in a grain wagon train revealed the means of distribution of plague.
  “Throughout her career, Dr. Green has combined humanism and science in ways that have brought a more clear understanding of the origins and spread of plague,” says Davis, from the Medieval Academy. “Her collaborations with historians, geneticists, paleobiologists, archaeologists and others untangle the genetic complexities of plague strains.”
  That kind of interdisciplinary work would have been significant to scholars at any moment, but right now takes on particular relevance. “[Green] has worked to undermine imprecise and simplistic plague narratives and to explain to a ready public the importance of understanding historic plagues in context,” adds Davis “[Her] voice has been critical as we try to make sense of our own modern-day plague.”
  Green also sees the relevance, especially as her study of plague variants and pandemic came out just as new variants of the Covid-19 pathogen were manifesting around the world. She tells me that her work didn’t change because of Covid, but the urgency did. “Plague,” Green says, “is our best ‘model organism’ for studying the history of pandemics because the history of it is now so rich, with the documentary and archaeological record being supplemented by the genetic record. All the work the virologists were doing in sequencing and tracking SARS-CoV-2's spread and genetic evolution was exactly the same kind of work that could be done for tracking  Yersinia pestis's evolution and movements in the past.”
  She wants her fellow scholars to focus on human agency both in history—those Mongols and their wagon trains—and now. The history of the Black Death tells “a powerful story of our involvement in creating this pandemic: this wasn't Mother Nature just getting angry with us, let alone fate. It was human activity.”
  The world is only now—thanks to Green and many others (see her long bibliography of scholars from a wide variety of disciplines, time periods, and parts of the world)—really getting a handle on the true history of the Black Death. Next, she tells me, she has an article coming out with Nahyan Fancy, a medieval Islamist, on further textual evidence of plague outbreaks to supplement the  Mongol News. Many of these 13th-century sources were previously known, but if you start with the assumption that the plague couldn’t be present until the 14th century, you’d never find them.
  She imagines scholars may find plague in other places, once they start looking. In the meantime, the stakes for understanding how diseases move remains crucial as we wrestle with our own pandemic. I ask her what she thinks it all means for a world today still grappling with a pandemic. She replies, with a harrowing, centuries-look ahead, “The story I have reconstructed about the Black Death is 100 percent an emerging infectious disease story. ... an ‘emerging’ disease lasted for 500-600 years!!!”
title: 比我们想到的全世界的黑色死亡横横蔓延？
title_en: Did the black death rampage across the world a century earlier than we thought?

note:
对于背景，早期投资者在初创企业之后，在关键调查中解除了联合创建者和流行的Youtuber，David Dobrik的指控之后。每个风险资本家我谈到，搬到“切断所有关系”的迁移才是前所未有的。
  那么这里的影响是什么？关于尽职调查的重要性，这是一个粗鲁的觉醒。在股权上，我认为DIMSO新闻应该在未来做出更加彻底的创始人做得更彻底的工作。 Dobrik是可疑的“恶作剧”总是搜索。
  尽管一个人不代表整个公司（Diseo的团队似乎很棒，但对于它的价值而言，因为他们的钱代表了什么。快速前进，此活动可能对与名人或影响者合作的VCS有冷却效果。责任似乎似乎太庞大而无法通过潜在的问题的人带领初创公司，所以要么停留或做作业。
 好吧，你会想。具有讽刺意味的是，在从初创公司支持的Dispo Investors后24小时是YC演示日，这是一年的Marquee启动事件之一。我的同事开玩笑说，创始人不仅仅需要弄清楚如何进入y组合者了 - 他们需要弄清楚如何在批处理中脱颖而出。在jest中制作的评论，强调了关于目前的启动资金环境的真相：太吵了处理。
  噪音变成了自由的投资。一位投资者从一家批量公司拿到了一封电子邮件，基本上，“谢谢你的兴趣，如果你想投资这是一个文件，无需尽职调查。”初创公司价值1亿美元。另一名投资者说，我发表谈到的是，一家公司在不符合VC的情况下要求投资。
 虽然这些只是轶事，但我认为这些音高是说明了尽职调查的重要性和我们所在的炒作周期之间的断开。随着Dispo向我们展示，它是兽医击中您未来的伴侣，然后恢复正确的初创公司并带来合适的钱。正如YC演示日向我们展示，当你快速时，很难走慢。如果钱在你面前晃来晃来，你怎么说不？
  我没有解决问题的解决方案，最终变化归结为个别投资者和创始人的精神。但至少，这一周的极端情况现在为疯狂的疯狂提供了一定的现实。 
在本通讯的其余部分，我们将专注于一个五个月的独角兽，并在不和谐的成本上的和谐。一如既往，你可以在Twitter @nmasc_上找到我。
   Pacaso是一个想要使人们更容易获得第二个居所拥有的初创公司，只需五个月就达到了10亿美元的估值。初学者基本上想要重新发明的时间，以“汇集一小组共同主任在一起购买一个家庭家庭”，玛丽安阿扎维德多报告。
 您可以每周六每周在您的收件箱中进行初创公司，所以在这里订阅加入酷孩子。
 这就是所知道的：Proptech Unicorns在这里留下来。我的同事埃里克·埃尔登关于房地产趋势，从共同生活到郊区风格的生活繁荣。
 如果你不相信我们：在这个最新的投资者调查中，Eldon调查了10个Pandemication和零售后的更好的ERA。
   即使是OL'企业巨头也希望提醒您社区问题。据报道，微软试图挖掘不和谐，在交易谈判中，后者将重视10亿美元。初创公司上次价值70亿美元。
 这里的知识：交易价格感觉略差，符合股权三重奏。当您认为格子花呢披肩几乎可以销售到签证时的格子才能估值，一个人不知道不和谐是否有反应折扣限制其定价。 
谈到合法头痛：罗宾情绪自信地向公众提起，这让我很兴奋！  以下是我们的TechCrunch早期会议的折扣码，我们为创始人，投资者和运营商的为期两天的虚拟活动。 使用代码“tcarticle”获得20％的票价，以便参加超酷的事件，如如何用Chalencly的Tope Avotona和OpenView的Blake Bartlett引导超酷的事件，如何与Kleiner Perkins'Cucky Moore一起推动您的系列筹款（由Moi主持 ）与Alexa von Tobel的创始人融资。  抓住超级早起的鸟类通过扰乱的人数不到100美元。 股权可能会做一些有趣和特别的特殊事项。  TechCrunch列表是根据创始人推荐的VC行业最活跃和参与投资者的目录。
note_en:
For background, early investors in Dispo distanced from the startup after a  key investigation unearthed allegations around co-creator and popular YouTuber, David Dobrik. Per venture capitalists I spoke to, the move to “sever all ties” with Dispo was unprecedented.
  So what’s the impact here? It’s a rude awakening on the importance of due diligence.  On Equity, I argued that the Dispo news should nudge venture capitalists to do a more thorough job with vetting founders in the future. Dobrik’s  questionable “pranks” were always a search away.
  Even though one person doesn’t represent an entire company (Dispo’s team  seems great, for what it’s worth), investors still left because of what their money represented. Fast forward, this event could have a chilling effect on VCs working with celebrities or influencers. The liability just seems too huge to back a startup led by potentially problematic individuals, so either stay away or do your homework.
 Well, you’d think. Ironically, 24 hours after Dispo investors backed away from the startup was YC Demo Day, one of the marquee startup events of the year. My colleague joked that founders don’t simply need to figure out how to get into Y Combinator anymore — they need to figure out how to stand out in the batch once they get there. The comment, made in jest, underscored a truth about the current startup funding environment: too noisy to handle.
  Noise turned into free-for-all investments. One investor  got an email from a batch company saying essentially, “thanks for your interest, if you want to invest here’s a document, no due diligence required.” The startup was valued at $100 million. Another investor I spoke to said that a company asked for an investment without meeting the VC.
 While these are only anecdotes, I think these pitches are illustrative of the disconnect between the importance of due diligence and the hype cycle we are in. As Dispo showed us, it’s net positive to vet your future partner, back the right startups and bring on the right money. As YC Demo Day showed us, it’s hard to go slow when you can go fast. If the money is dangling in front of you, how do you say no?
  I don’t have a solution to the disconnect, and ultimately the change comes down to the ethos of individual investors and founders. But at minimum, this week of extremes gives a dose of reality to startup mania right now.
 In the rest of this newsletter, we’ll focus on a five-month unicorn, and Plaid’s harmony at Discord’s cost. As always, you can find me  on Twitter @nmasc_.
   Pacaso, a startup that wants to make it easier for people to have second home ownership, has  reached a $1 billion valuation in just five months. The startup essentially wants to reinvent timeshares, with the goal of “bringing together a small group of co-owners to purchase a share of a single-family home” with access throughout the year,  Mary Ann Azevedo reports.
 You can get Startups Weekly in your inbox every Saturday,  so subscribe here to join the cool kids.
 Here’s what to know: The proptech unicorns are here to stay. My colleague  Eric Eldon wrote about real estate trends,  from co-living to a suburban-style living boom.
 And if you don’t believe us: In this latest investor survey, Eldon surveyed  10 proptech investors who see a better era for residential and retail after the pandemic.
   Even an ol’ enterprise giant wants to remind you that  community matters. Microsoft is reportedly trying to scoop up Discord,  in deal talks that would value the latter at $10 billion. The startup was last valued at $7 billion.
 Here’s what to know: The deal price feels slightly cheap,  argues the Equity trio. When you consider the fact that Plaid could be valued at almost double or triple for what it was going to be sold to Visa, one has to wonder if Discord has an anti-trust discount limiting its pricing.
 Speaking of legal headaches:  Robinhood filed confidently to go public, which, sue me, makes me excited as heck!
   Here’s a discount code to our TechCrunch Early Stage conference, our two-day virtual event for founders, investors and operators. Use code  “TCARTICLE” to get 20% off your ticket so you can attend super-cool events like how to bootstrap with Calendly’s Tope Awotona and OpenView’s Blake Bartlett, how to pitch your Series A fundraise with Kleiner Perkins’ Bucky Moore (moderated by moi) and finance for founders with Alexa von Tobel.
 Grab super early-bird passes to TechCrunch Disrupt  for less than $100.  Equity might do something fun and special, who knows.

Damon Krukowski和他的妻子开了娜奥米杨。他们是80年代后期的三分之二的Indie Rock Band Galaxie 500，通过东欧巡回作为梦幻般的流行装备，称为Damon＆amp; Naomi。经过多年的战争在前南斯拉夫，贝尔格莱德仍处于经济制裁，并脱离了网格;科技公司无法访问街道地图。 “我们进入了非法人的世界，出于政治原因，”克劳克斯基告诉我“。
  他不得不在纸上找到一个付费电话并在纸上写下指示，追游早期的粗暴返回。场地落后于抵制Milošević制度的广播电台。当Damon＆amp; Naomi占据了舞台并开始发挥，在一个城市被切断的一条乐队中的一个乐队，克鲁克斯基意识到房间里的每个人都知道他们的歌曲。
    “这是如此移动，但这是关于盗版，”他说。塞尔维亚广播电台下载了Damon＆amp; Naomi的文件和广播它们，创建粉丝底座。 Krukowski并没有真正有问题。 “谁能抱怨他们的音乐能够达到没有购买它的人的人？”他问。 “这太棒了，我们可以演奏一个节目。”
  这是销往数百万音乐的故事，这是互联网的全球范围。在一个关联的世界，想法，思想和是的，歌曲可能会突破边界，建立在存在之前没有的受众。这是一个梦想对自己的独立艺术家的人类的梦想呼吁。他们可以玩他们想要的音乐，找到自己的利基和茁壮成长。它甚至可能会改变世界。
  与我们经济的许多部门一样，政府无所作为使音乐业务巩固，对音乐家的毁灭性影响。
  但是，成功的承诺很快就遇到了数字时代的现实。 Krukowski不再沉迷于此幻想了。 “我们正在汇集行业中的更多钱，更少，手少，”他说。 “它不是设计为民主化音乐或使一百万人成为一个生活。这只是一个少数少数，它正在萎缩。“
  音乐行业曾经爬过足够庞大的艺术家。但是今天垄断者和寡头人的各方都困扰着艺术家。与我们经济的许多部门一样，政府无所作为使音乐业务巩固，对音乐家的毁灭性影响。 
收音机是一个公司手中的令人震惊的程度，自由媒体。两家公司，直播国家和ANSCHUTZ娱乐集团（AEG），控制大量场地和艺术家管理服务，与实时国家主导票务。主要标签已经削减到三个。记录商店，alt-weeklies和培养当地音乐场景的其他元素在很大程度上。
  尽管为绝大多数艺术家提供了挑留，但妄想这一切都是媒体的流动，这已成为行业的主要收入来源。对于主要的流媒体公司 -  Youtube和Spotify-Music真的是一个丢失领导者，附带数据收集，可以从那种数据销售的广告，以及观众增长到投资者的承诺。 “Spotify是从驾驶粉丝的平台上的每位艺术家受益，”克里斯堡，曾在A＆amp的娱乐律师录音。 “标签说他们给你曝光。这条线是你可以死去的曝光。“
  您的捐款将免费和打开此网站以阅读。给你能...
 这种行业的商业模式的激进上台使得一些明星有益，而中等收入艺术家，如美国的中产阶级，努力生存。数字录音工具和社交媒体的无处不在掩盖这种痛苦;感觉像音乐一样充满活力。在他们走了之前，难以辨别艺术家的痛苦。
  大流行者残忍地带来了这个家。追求旅游的表演者看到他们的生计蒸发。有些退出业务;其他人陷入沉默。但是发生了有趣的事情。在道路上互相传递的音乐家开始组织如何确保公平赔偿他们的工作。
  “我们正在努力将艺术家的需求和权利持续进入谈话，”比基尼杀人杀戮记录和董事会成员的音乐家，艺术家权利联盟的董事会成员，玛吉·沃尔·沃尔·沃尔·沃尔（Maggie Vail）说。 “如果你没有健康的音乐家，你就没有健康的行业。”
  “你可以去黄金，仍然欠记录标签......”-ice-t 
音乐“业务”的概念相对较新。几个世纪以来，工作音乐家需要惠顾，通过教会或贵族的皇室或表演。 19世纪批量生产乐谱允许金钱以更广泛的流量，这与托马斯爱迪生的发明扩大了留声机的发明，后来Guglielmo Marconi的收音机。音乐家可以记录，包装和销售歌曲作为商品，而不是赚钱，而不是刚玩过的生活。
  拜占庭式系统演变为获得歌曲犯罪者的特许权限，无论何处音乐都在记录上，在电台上，在电影，俱乐部或酒吧或零售店或电梯。绩效权利组织（专业人士）收集这些版税，并将资金分发给版权持有人。自20世纪40年代以来，司法部和占主导地位的司法司和ASCAP之间的同意法令试图确保所有艺术家的公平版税治疗。
  但艺术家需要首先听到他们的音乐。 “你有数千家商店和物理产品，”跨国公司是大西洋记录总统，后来的独立标签艺术家。 “你必须有重大的分销，以将记录纳入商店，而不是背部的商店。”
   戈德伯格将记录标签描述为“艺术家职业生涯的投资银行家”。标签将前往生产和营销的成本，并使艺术家的艺术家抵御性能特许权使用费（有两种特许权使用费，一个用于歌曲性绩效，而且一个用于潜在的表现;因为曲案特许权使用费与标签进步分开，作家和出版商造成更多钱）。一些标签甚至为旅游支持提供资金。一记录将在其他歌手和乐队上融资。
  从一开始，该系统导致开发，正如艺术与商业碰撞的情况一样，如此。标签有明确的信息优势：他们控制了书籍，并确定了利润和费用。歪曲唱片历史悠久的悲伤史，黑艺术家们的音乐撕掉并卖给白众观众，顶部乐队只看到他们的前进，因为标签必须“收回”成本。第一笔交易总是糟糕，猎人会争取重新谈判。但是，婚姻有点忍受的是可用的洪水。记录是现金牛，粉丝不断将他们的目录更新为盒式磁带和光盘等新技术。销量增加了三十年;在20世纪90年代的巅峰时期，音乐每年的音乐为400亿美元。
  您的捐款将免费和打开此网站以阅读。给你能...
 尽管如此，总是有一个暗流的叛乱。 1994年，珍珠果酱申请诉诸TicketMaster，以利用其主导地位在售票粉丝票务中。对来自朋克和替代场景流动的主要标签和企业岩石的谴责，即使其中一些人最终与专业签名。 “在丑陋的丑陋之后，总会有一个次级音乐业务，”Singer-Songwriter Kristin Hersh（投掷缪斯，50footwave）说。 “当我们被允许支付租金并继续参与我们激情时，地下职能。” 
对于独立艺术家来说，经济学很小，但可以理解。车库中专业质量录音所允许的低成本工具。您可以按自己的CD，并在节目中销售上述成本。独立分销商，标签和录音商店可以允许全国遵循。你在家乡和在其他地方联网，通过志同道合的场地和社区广播和风扇Zines联系在其他地方。 “我们在主流文化之外拥有强大的行业，”玛吉·沃尔（Maggie Vail）表示，在杀戮摇滚明星，这是一个独立的标签，该标签是Elliott Smith，Sliatel-Kinney和12名12名12位儿主义者。艺术家并没有致富，但他们可以播放音乐并支付租金，也许会休息一下，并获得全国。
      我对这个故事的许多艺术家都没有被档案共享的早期被动。他们将其与超级携带感激的死亡表演或歌曲的超级携带。但有些人甚至看到了它的承诺。 “我就像，这不是很大的，但哇，我们现在可以直接分发给我们的粉丝，”大卫·洛菲诺·帕特·瓦·贝多芬和后来饼干的歌手说。 “也许足够的人会支付网络结果是我们作为艺术家的报酬。”
  然而，标签翻了出来，有充分的理由。 “单打和MP3格式的数字分布将拖欠专辑，”斯蒂芬·威特，音乐如何免费获得。 “想想像'冰冰宝宝这样的歌。'他们卖了2000万相册。专辑中有14首歌曲，没有人听他们。“如果他们想要听到的唯一歌曲，您就无法让人们为CD支付15美元的CD。
  虽然Limewire和Kazaa这样的模仿者持久地持续了几年的几年。标签将它们取得了不存在，但犹豫不决，提供了即时音乐下载的替代品。同时，2001年，Apple发布了iPod。 “如果音乐盗版是锅贸易，那么苹果就是蒸发器，”威特说。 iPod有15,000首歌曲的存储，但没有人拥有许多歌曲。正如Witt解释的那样，“模型是，去偷走一切并将其加载到你的iPod上。”
  在拒绝丢失他们有利可图的商业模式的年份，否则的标签需要解决方案。因此，他们向Apple的iTunes商店授权每次跟踪音乐销售。 “iTunes看起来就像Napster一样，但添加了一列的列，这是”99美分“，”Damon Krukowski说。 “Napster的窗口是为盗版而设计的。它只是标题，艺术家和文件大小。 iTunes增加了价格。没有信用，没有服务音乐社区的信息。“
  YouTube是世界上最大的音乐流媒体平台，占全球各项按需游戏时间的47％。
  随着专辑不捆绑，记录公司收入开始了一个15年的萧条，筛选到艺术家。标签合并，寻求市场份额安全。多年来有一个大五个;然后索尼买了BMG和普遍购买了EMI，缩小到2016年的三分之二的音乐市场的三分之二。 （华纳音乐是另一个。） 
录制和营销预算开始倾斜。进展减少。促销人员被解雇了。标签发明了“360交易”，除了他们可以从合成权利中恢复什么时，将他们占据了艺术家的所有收入。这很快成为行业标准。通过将手放在他们的艺术家口袋里，该标签补偿了专辑的死亡。
  “我就像一个龙头，垄断是对象......” - 根源
  Apple的数字分销杀死了记录商店，任何当地音乐场景中的节点。物理销售成为恐龙，限于感情者和收藏家。 Alt-Weeklies的并发衰落，其中精选音乐列表和访谈并显示评论，造成类似的伤害。专注的音乐杂志也遭受了削减。 “发生的第一件事是我们在小型出版物中停止广告，”Vail说。 “只是辐射出来。”
  收音机是达到粉丝的另一种方式也在转变。 1996年的电信法允许整合跨场无线电，两个大网络，积云和清晰的渠道，主导电视。 Sirius和XM在2000年代初推出了卫星无线网络，然后彼此合并。在集中的无线电景观中，格式缩小，不同的艺术家无法突破，当地内容变得不存在。当地站成为幽灵城镇;远程办公楼中的程序员确定了播放列表，机器人更换了DJ。
  即使是那种浓度水平也没有工作。积云和清晰的频道，现在被称为iheartmedia，陷入破产。一家公司拿起碎片。自由媒体购买了Siriusxm，然后购买了一个在线广播公司的Pandora。自由在现场，场地和票务巨头中有三分之一的股份。在2020年，它在Iheartmedia进行了控制股权，拥有850个AM / FM站（比任何其他公司超过任何其他公司）和国家最大的在线广播网络和商业播客生产商。他们正在推动对当地无线电所有权的限制进一步。
  一家公司这家公司在垂直整合，结合地面，卫星和在线广播，流媒体，艺术家管理，票务，场地和音乐会促销，使AirPlay成为所有但少数艺术家的不可能性。 “此时，这是我忽视的标签，”Vail说。 “我不会花时间或努力推动。”
  代替培养艺术家是互联网的离线网络，作为风扇发现和艺术家访问的连接点。那里有一个真相的内核。 Pitchfork和音乐博客打开了奇怪的流派和利基艺术家，而MySpace和后来的Facebook给了一个家里的家。如果您收集了社交媒体用户，您可以向他们留言即将到来的节目和记录销售。但早期转变为网络如何不可避免地改变。 “Facebook做了这个疯狂的诱饵和开关，”Lowery说。 “我们建立了我们可以访问朋友的乐队页面。但是当我们发布到我们的页面时，他们没有向粉丝展示。然后我们必须开始支付Facebook以访问我们的粉丝。“ 
这证明了艺术家如何在游泳池的深处趟过，在那里有很多关于他们的生计会摆脱他们的控制。 “互联网初期的言论是胜利的废话，”阿斯特拉泰勒，一位作家，电影制片人和活动家，丈夫，杰夫曼节，前往Lo-Fi Rock Band中性牛奶饭店。 （阿斯特拉偶尔会与集团一起玩。）“没有任何政治经济学。十年前对我来说显而易见的是Spotify的东西是最终的，没有像文化民主。“
  这正是发生了什么。克里斯城堡可以看到它来到它的时候，他抓住了被运作作为原始流服务的重新启动版的Napster版本的广告。标语是：无所事事，拥有一切。城堡召回：“我认为那里，那是结束。”
    David Lowery，现在在佐治亚大学讲课除了制作音乐之外，将互联网描述为重新组装所有护理家远离公平赔偿的守门员。 “我们庆祝了Dimintermiation，并经历了一个重新中介的过程，”他说。
  这在2005年与YouTube开始，一个存储库，几乎所有录制的音乐，都可以免费访问宽带连接的任何人。 YouTube鼓励用户发布内容，几乎立即包含歌曲。在谷歌购买YouTube之前，2006年仅为16.5亿美元，所谓的网站是“内容盗窃的流氓推动者”。谷歌估计，所有YouTube视图中有60％的人都是“高级”版权物质。
   谷歌的投资受到数字千年版权法案，第512节的保护，该公司授予来自检方的安全港，该公司的用户发布受版权保护的材料，只要他们在被问及时取消侵权内容的过程。但是对于像youtube一样大量的平台，“通知和抛售”是徒劳无益的运动。每分钟将五百小时的视频上传到YouTube。一旦你花一个下来，另一个弹出了另一个。 “YouTube有一种保护计划，”Bob Dylan和乐队前的旅游经理Jon Taplin说。 “他们说，'记录公司，您的内容将在此处在这里升级是否喜欢。”
  YouTube确实运行称为内容ID的服务，该服务将上传的视频与主要标签提供的现有版权材料进行比较，并自动取下副本。但是较小的艺术家从内容ID系统中莫名其妙地禁止。 “大型创造者与资源的权利以自己的资源接受自己的法院受到保护，”读取了去年提交的YouTube的课程诉讼诉讼，“较小和独立的创造者......故意在寒冷中遗忘。”该诉讼辩称，这款双层系统应使YouTube的安全港无效。它仍在诉讼中。
  虽然并不总是在这种方式思考时，YouTube是世界上最大的音乐流平台，占全球各通各事的47％，据2018年报告称号的音乐剧行业（IFPI），一个非营利组织贸易小组。大多数视图通过基于专有算法的连续自动播放流循环，从专辑中取消统一。这倾向于集中在播放的集中，因为youtube致力于可靠的人群，以保持人们上瘾。 “它成为一个胜利者的所有业务，”Taplin说。 “你在底部得到了这个拥挤，让他们的母亲和女朋友倾听他们的音乐，就是这样。” 
YouTube的复杂和不透明的支付结构基于各种因素，包括一天中的时间，听众的位置，并与流绑定的广告收入。但是返回艺术家的平均资金几乎无法计算出来。 Trichordist的最新流定价圣经中的估计显示YouTube的内容ID，主要与主要标签连接的歌曲，每条流量产生0.00022美元。结合货币化视频的上传者的利率，您可以更接近克里斯城堡的克里斯城堡议会委员会，每条汇集0.00074美元。此次速度，10,000条流 - 这是10,000名不同的人，聆听艺术家的音乐 - 转化为大约7美元，少于iTunes上的一张专辑的价格。
  Maria Schneider是一位胜利的爵士乐和古典艺术家，并在YouTube诉讼中引导原告。 “他们推动了这个话语，艺术家可以通过把广告施加到他们身上来改善他们的作品，”她说。 “我和我知道的人谈过，这是非常聪明的人。当我告诉他们钱真的看起来像什么，他们只是震惊，他们不知道。“
  您肯定可以将YouTube视为现场表演的促销考虑因素。该公司不断宣传关于艺术家的成功案例，归功于其溪流闯入。甚至灰化的退伍军人也可以受益。 Stephen Witt讲述了他对他与旅行搭配的杰罗夫库特突然讨论的谈话。 “他在抱怨他有一些歌曲，尤Youtube宣传了3亿，他赚了30美元，”威特说。 “但是，明年，Portishead将Glastonbury Festival全身。他们在十年内没有专辑。我说，'杰夫，如果youtube不在，你就不会拥有这个标题演出。“
  如果流媒体没有像音乐销售等其他收入，那么叙述更容易吞下，如果YouTube的母公司谷歌不是这个星球上最有价值和最有价值的贪婪的企业之一。谷歌将所有数据从YouTube收集并使用其他信息捕获它捕获的其他数据，使用该信息以目标广告。艺术家不分享他们对数据收集的贡献;这笔钱是在他们的视频之外生成的，有时会关闭YouTube网站
